Unlike the larger bug zappers, this one doesn’t seem quite powerful enough to kill most bugs with one zap. And in addition to that, the bugs seem to get zapped multiple times because of its design. With most bug zappers the bugs get zapped once, and they fall into a tray at the base of the zapper. With this bug zapper, they get zapped multiple times as they tumble through its architecture of wires. Often as they are tumbling through its wires, they literally catch on fire and they are often alive through part of this process. So what you end up getting with this product is this cacophony of sparks as an insect tumbles through the wires buzzing loudly for a large part of that time and then as it literally catches on fires and burns, you get this awful smell that you can smell 30 feet away. As I said before with a typical bug zapper the bugs get zapped one time and then they fall to a tray at the base of the zapper and that’s it. There’s no fire, there’s no multiple zaps there’s no ridiculously horrible smell filling the area. All that being said, I started to research bug zappers after the horrible performance of this one and I found out that they don’t even do very well in zapping mosquitoes or biting flies. Those type of bugs just aren’t very attracted to them. They actually end up zapping a lot of moths and other harmless bugs. If you’re trying to trap mosquitoes or flies, there are bug traps and repellents specifically built for them, and I would suggest you get those instead.

reviewTitle: READ BEFORE YOU BUY! Looks good but design is flawed.
reviewDescription: This zapper looks nice, but it doesn't work well. The biggest problem is the design. The white plastic rim sticks out farther than the metal part that actually zaps bugs. It's also curved, so when you try to press it against a wall or floor, the zapper part doesn’t even touch the bug. I included a video where I tried to zap a spider on the wall, but the spider kept walking, totally unbothered. I stopped recording just so I could grab my slipper and smack it because the zapper was doing absolutely nothing. This zapper only works if you swing it through the air and hit a bug mid-flight. Even when you do manage to swing and hit something, small bugs like fruit flies usually don’t get zapped and slip through. Super frustrating. Also, the built-in light is useless. It doesn’t attract bugs well. When I’m sitting outside with the zapper nearby, bugs still fly around me and attack my face. TL;DR: Looks nice but not worth buying. Doesn’t do the job and bugs are still terrorizing me.

reviewTitle: Will not kill a fly with one shot
reviewDescription: Definitely will NOT kill a fly on the first hit or with a simple swat. You'll hear an absurdly loud zap, but you'll be lucky if it even stuns them...most of the time they just keep on flying. The only way to kill a fly with this is to trap it against a wall or window so it hits the mesh a few times, or if you're lucky enough to have a fly land on the racket while it's lying flat on a table. You'll need at least 4-5 zaps to actually kill the fly, and you get to enjoy the smell of burning fly at that point. Might be more effective with mosquitoes but for all the noise this thing makes, it's pretty amazing that flies just bounce off it like it's nothing.

reviewTitle: Zevo Flying Insect Traps & 2 Refill Cartridges (first and second version)
reviewDescription: My two‑star rating reflects an average between the first and second versions of the Zevo trap, because the two products performed very differently. First Version – No Longer Available, and Misleading The original version is no longer listed, so I can’t leave a review specifically for it. If I could, it wouldn’t get more than one star. The main reason I bought it was the claim that it also worked for mosquitoes. After using it for a year and a half, I can confirm that this was completely false. It catches plenty of other flying insects, but never mosquitoes, which made the original purchase pointless for my needs. Second Version – Accurate Description, Works Properly The updated version no longer claims to trap mosquitoes—likely removed after customer complaints. And this time, the product performs exactly as described. For the insects it lists, it works reliably, and I would normally rate this version five stars on its own. Final Rating Logic Because I can’t leave a separate review for the discontinued first version, and because the original misleading claims were the reason I bought the product in the first place, I’m forced to average the experience. I can’t give 2.5 stars, so I’m leaving 2 stars to reflect the accurate performance of the new version and the misleading marketing of the old one.

I gave Zevo these two out of five stars because this trap only catches 2 out of 5 Gnats. I placed two devices where Gnats love to gather. One in the Kitchen, One near the bathroom. I check every day, as I also kill Gnats by hand every day. I have a very clean home; I use a garbage disposal and do not store food that can rot in the kitchen bin. I store all fruit and vegetables in the refrigerator. Yet every time that I leave a door or window open for fresh air, these little critters gather. (Even with screens). I have tried everything. But this was the most expensive and least successful. I will return to flying insect sprays. I do not know what so called "Genius" thought that insects like "blue light." But I do know, he/she is not very intelligent. I also have 2 blue light/ light bulbs, that are also zappers... also no results. My recommendations, keep a clean house, wash and dry all fruit and vegetables and store them appropriately, (never store fruit or any food in open air). And my latest discovery... If you use a citrus dish cleaning liquid, keep the bottle clean and the top tight. It is weird, but I have recently discovered that Gnats are attracted to the artificial scent.

reviewTitle: It just didn't attract the flying insects as much as was advertised, especially for the price.
reviewDescription: It's basically expensive fly paper. The blue light is supposed to attract flying bugs and it does - but only mildly. The height of fruit fly season runs from August to October. There were so many fruit flies in my house, it was hard to have a conversation without waiving the little buggers away. Enter Zevo - which I have seen being advertised aggressively on TV as being the greatest flying insect eradicator ever. So I shelled out the money for 2 blue lit insect traps and 2 refill cartridges, assembled, plugged in and sat back waiting for the fruit fly fog to clear. It never did. I broke down and added 2 bowls filled with Apple Cider Vinegar, topped with tightly covered saran wrap, poked some tiny holes in the wrap and watched to see what worked better. Scot 1 for the Apple Cider Vinegar. I collected 100x more fruit flies in 2 days than I did the entire time I had the cartridges plugged in. The blue lights are pretty - I'll give it that. But it's passively effective, not aggressively effective, so I'll continue to use my tried and true, albeit kinda gross, method.

reviewTitle: More bug nightclub than bug graveyard.
reviewDescription: It’s gnat season, and after yet another round of vinegar pots, I decided to go high tech with these Zevo plug-in traps. I put one upstairs, one downstairs, and waited for the carnage. In practice? Not much carnage. After a month (and a house so thick with gnats you could smack them out of the air just walking by), I’ve caught… maybe 15. I watch the bugs chilling in the blue light like it’s happy hour, but they refuse to land on the sticky trap. Maybe my gnats are too smart, maybe my walls are extra tasty, or maybe I’m just more attractive than UV. Whatever it is, these things aren’t worth the money or the hassle of a bright blue beacon glowing 24/7 on both floors of my house. To be fair: The design is pretty cool. The sticky refills are easy to replace, and you don’t have to touch the goo. But I could easily catch more bugs with my bare hands in three minutes than these have snagged in three weeks. If the point is to catch bugs, this thing missed the mark. Unless you’re looking to start your own insect sanctuary, I’d keep looking.

reviewTitle: Not the solution that I've been told.
reviewDescription: Check the review pictures. It catches a bunch of fruit flies, some midges, a couple of mosquitoes, and in my case, one enterprising moth. But despite having a bunch of houseflies in my house and this plug being very visible for almost two weeks, NO HOUSEFLIES CAUGHT. I had a friend that raved about this catching all their flies. And despite what I considered a high price tag for a disposable-cartridge type product I went for it. Because we have a ton of flies (it's fall and I live in a rural area.) About a week after this has been in my kitchen, I also put up a traditional strip of flypaper which now has a dozen flies on it. Still no houseflies in the zevo. I'm on the verge of saying one star, but I'll give it two because it does catch a bunch of fruit flies that I didn't know we're so bad.

reviewTitle: After using since late last summer till now, Save your money and buy a bug/animal glue pad catcher
reviewDescription: Purchased this over 6 months ago and have tried them in different rooms, areas, and heights, and I don't see them doing anything great. I had them in a spot for over a month and maybe one or two little noes see em bugs land on one by the door. Flies don't go on them I have watched them land or scurry right around it like it had no interest at all to them. Also, the way it is curved like a quarter moon is so close to the wall when you plug it in I don't think any bugs will go behind the cover then jump onto the pad. Most of the time there is never anything on them after a month even. It reminds me of those ways you catch a mouse glue pad type that is put in with a light. I am going to buy the mouse catch pads and put a night light by it. I think I will have better luck with that or a fly bug strip. And they don't last that long before you have to replace the pad and the price for the couple of replacements is way over priced.

I set up both devices in my kitchen and bathroom where I’ve been having issues with fruit flies, but after several days, there was barely any sign of flies getting trapped. The UV light seems weak, and the sticky pads didn’t catch much of anything. I followed all the instructions and even moved the devices around to different spots, but the results were still minimal. The design is nice and it’s quiet, which I appreciate, but that doesn’t help much when the fruit flies are still buzzing around. Might work better in a completely dark space or with a smaller infestation, but for me, it just didn’t do the job. Disappointed overall.

I bought a bug zapper for the kitchen, which worked well, but scares the heck out of you every time a bug gets zapped. Sam's also had a rotating bug trap that works pretty well. When the gnats moved to the bathroom, there wasn't enough space away from the sink, toilet, or bathtub to put the zapper. I went ahead and bought these traps to plug in there, as well as the spray that Zevo sells. I figured, given the small footprint of the bathroom, the plug-in trap would be perfect. The first night, it caught two gnats, and I thought, "Great, it works!" However, a week later, the same two gnats were in there, and no additional gnats. Another week, same two gnats. For whatever reason, the "night light" lure just didn't bring in any more gnats. I ended up killing the rest of the gnats either with the spray or with a swatter of some type (mostly my hand). Does it work? Sure, yes. Does it work well? Not really, at least not in my case. I did notice that both Sam's and Costco sell these as well, so I'm guessing they have to be popular enough, right?

Works better than just leaving it plugged in 😅 - If that's the case, you are better off wrapping sticky tape and with some of the sticky side up, around a fly swatter at that point.

Although, it does attract bugs, they are few and far between. reviewTitle: After Comparison Test, sticking with Zevo Name Brand refills
reviewDescription: I bought both this Baimnocm off-brand refill pack as well as some Zevo name brand refills for my Zevo Insect Traps. I put this Baimnocm off-brand refill out where I knew there were bugs, checked back next morning and there were only two insects stuck to trap. However, I could see numerous bugs walking all over the “sticky” surface then fly away without any harm and definitely not trapped. My initial success with Zevo (including Zevo brand sticky trap portion) was excellent, so I wanted to compare To compare the two refills, I set up a trap with a Zevo name brand refill right next to the trap with the Baimnocm refill. I then watched bugs walking all over all over the Baimnocm “sticky” portion of the trap refill, fly off, land on the Zevo sticky portion of the refill and get stuck! I left them together for a day for a more complete comparison. After 24 hours, the Zevo refill sticky trap had 42 bugs trapped, whereas the Baimnocm refill trap had 3 measly bugs (including the 2 that had been trapped the day before). I read reviews where others had success with the off-brand refills and reviews where people had problems with the off-brand refills. Perhaps the off brand Baimnocm refills are just terribly inconsistent in quality—my Baimnocm trap refill batch certainly was poor quality. But my experience with the Zevo products has been that they work great every time. I’m sticking with the Zevo name brand refills. They may be more expensive, but the work well and consistently. The Baimnocm refill really didn’t even work— I think that’s more expensive, paying good money for something that just doesn’t deliver.

reviewTitle: You can catch more fruit flies with vinegar than you can with ZEVO traps.
reviewDescription: My garden has been overflowing with tomatoes and summer squash and my counters were accumulating bowls of produce faster than I could can or freeze them. Suddenly I had an infestation of fruit flies. Prior to using ZEVO, I was using an old home remedy for trapping fruit flies. I placed a piece of banana in a jar, covered the opening with plastic wrap, and punched a couple of slits in the plastic wrap. In a second jar I placed pieces of tomato. The fruit flies can get into the jar through the slits but can’t get out. My ZEVO order arrived two days after I set out the jars and I eagerly put the ZEVO traps to work. The next morning I checked the traps. The ZEVO traps had only 5 or 6 fruit flies attached to the sticky trap. The jars which had been sitting out for 2 days, had a dozen or so fruit fries and my counters were still full of flies. The next morning, the ZEVO traps had a few more flies but the jars were full of flies. Now, four days later, the ZEVO traps continue to underperform. I replaced the banana with a little vinegar and it’s like a fruit fly magnet.

reviewTitle: Did not work for fruit flies- waste of our money
reviewDescription: So far I am not at all impressed. Not sure how we got them but all of a sudden we had a ton of fruit flies. (Despite no fruit out and rotting. Nothing we could find. Unless they were in the drain….i have no clue where they came from) We did a kitchen and fridge deep clean. We did drain cleaning. Garbage can bleaching. Etc. Bought these. Plugged one in in out kitchen. A few days later…nothing. Not one single fruit fly was caught. Opened and plugged in the second one as well. Two days later….still nothing. I killed more with my hands than these caught. I gave up and called my mom and ended up doing the old wives take ACV and dish soap with plastic wrap and holes in it over it. I have 10 over night that way. Two days later they are all dead in my ACV trap. Too bad these $35 traps couldn’t have caught and killed a single one. Maybe they work better with different bugs than fruit flies but….they absolutely did nothing for us.

reviewTitle: Zevo fly trap did not work for me
reviewDescription: Many reviewers praise this fly trap and have had success with it, but my experience has been that the fly trap is very ineffective. I have numerous house flies in my condo, but they ignore the Zevo fly trap and do not seem attracted to the UV light. I have tried placing the fly trap right where the flies are, tried dimming the lights and darkening the room to make the UV light more visible, and have tried moving the trap around to several different locations. None of these approaches work. At the moment, there are approximately 10 flies in my small condo. They fly around and near the trap, but ignore it and do not seem attracted to it at all. For me, this was a fairly expensive investment to help control the flies. I was optimistic it would help based on all the positive reviews, but it did not work for me. I'm already calling it a loss and looking for another alternative.

reviewTitle: What are you really paying for/ getting?
reviewDescription: It’s not exactly what it represents is in the box, at least the one I got. Now my box had been torn open, so I may be wrong. Yes there was 2 traps, but no pads were in them, so the 2 “refill pads” actually should be a part of the traps, so no refill pads are part of the package!

I’d give it 0 Stars if possible. reviewTitle: Old fashioned remedy wins the day and smokes the Flying Insect Traps.
reviewDescription: Over the course of three days using these in hopes of dealing with an infestation of gnats, the one in the bathroom caught a gnat and a mosquito. Then, someone suggested I simply put some apple cider vinegar in a glass, with a drop of Dawn dish soap, rubber band a cover of plastic wrap and punch some holes in it. I did this in both the bathroom and the kitchen. In two days in the bathroom, I caught six gnats. In the same period in the kitchen, I caught more than I could count - probably 50 or more. The flying insect traps are still plugged in but haven't caught any additional gnats (not too many around since I put out the apple cider vinegar.

We have lots of gnats flying around in our house. Though this would be better than a bug bomb. We bought the two pack. 2 days and not a single catch. The device does absolutely nothing to attract bugs. I suspect the blue light is just a standard blue LED light. Our old fashioned TAT fly strips are out performing these things at this point. I’m sure one of them will eventually catch a bug, when it happens to fly between the shield and the wall. Until then it looks cool in the dark. * Update * I moved them around the house. I have two Zevos. I’ve had them for just over a month. It total we have captured 4 bugs. In the same amount of time. I’ve killed two with a fly swatter, two with toilet paper and one by clapping my hands. Not super impressed with them. I still think the light is just for show.

Do not get it from this seller it arrived broken. I had inspected the Zevo on arrival and plugged it in and it doesn’t light up because who ever put it together didn’t seat the white outer shell to the gray housing cover. There is an opening between them and I bought a two pack so it’s obvious one light up and the other one doesn’t. It’s so irritating I have to go out of my way and ship it back to them. I wish they had inspected it but it probably was a return and they didn’t even check it when they put it back for sale.

What did staples do with it??? Now I am out 40 dollars and I'm out the worthless product. UPS is the only way to return. Don't buy zevo

reviewTitle: Did not help with my fly problem
reviewDescription: I bought this hoping to solve a problem with houseflies. Our place is full of them and I don't know where they are coming from. I have searched and could not find a place where they might be breeding. I didn't rely on just Amazon reviews to buy this. It has reviews all over the net saying that it worked well for the kind of problem I have. When I received this I read the instructions and put it up in an area that is full of flies. It has been up for a couple of weeks and has only caught maybe half a dozen flies. There are still tons of flies zooming around in here doing what flies do. They buzz my face, attack my eyes and mouth, land on my ears and sit there buzzing. I'm still trying to find the source where they are coming from but this product isn't helping. It said it would help me combat houseflies but the flies aren't interested in it. The blue light was supposed to attract them. I don't think it does. I don't know if it works for other flying insects but it does not help with houseflies. I saw reviews that made it seem like a near miracle worker. It is not. Do your research and look beyond review sites before you buy this product.

Situation: Fruit flies had gotten out of hand in the kitchen and other parts of the house. Tried other home remedies with mixed results Got the Zevo and well…i’s a nice night light. That’s it. Over the course of around three weeks, sitting in my kitchen, it caught and killed 4-5 fruit flies. It was easy to assemble and set up, but it just didn’t work as expected and especially in regards to price point. Save your money. I was using one of those Terro Apples and a Raid Yellow fly stick (with apple cider vinegar poured in the bottom of it) and this is the order of effectiveness: 1) Raid yellow stick is the best for fruit flies. 2) Terro Apple is effective over the long haul but if you need to kill quickly, it’s Raid. 3) Zevo is shiny and neat looking and it’s a decent night light but it only killed around 3-4 fruit flies and did that mostly at night.

reviewTitle: Disappointed - did not perform as advertised

reviewTitle: Edit: Possible fire hazard. They work well and seem to be pretty kid and pet safe
reviewDescription: Edit: I plugged it into a charge port that was USBC PD capable and it seems that it didn't negotiate it's power level down because in about 30 seconds, the zapper had become the zapee.. It made a horrible smell and got *extremely* hot... Surprisingly it was still working for a couple of days after, but that charge was the last charge it would ever take. I'm lucky I didn't plug it in and leave or it would have almost certainly caught fire and possibly burned the house down. Aside from that they're pretty good. I'm probably going to buy another to replace it and just be concious of where and how I charge it. I'll probably charge it outside or in the all cement (even the ceiling) garage from now on. I have put a serious dent into the fly population in just a few days now... The rackets recharge nicely with USB-C and if you like, you can just leave them on all the time with the charge wire in and they'll stay charged and keep zapping thing constantly. That said, they don't seem to attract flies with their light very well, but I have seen very few things that do. They do occasionally get something on their own though. The battery life seems fantastic so far. I don't really know what it is because I've never seen them get below a full charge indicated since their initial charge, but I've only had them off of the charger and powered up for about 1/2 an hour or so before, so I really don't know... but they seem like they can't be too bad. I'm sure if someone tried hard enough they'd manage to zap themselves with the racket, but I've intentionally touched the screen to see if their claim was true or not, and I was not zapped... I'd say it would probably be really common to zap yourself through incidental/accidental contact. I'm sure that makes it a *little* bit harder to zap a fly out of the air, but it's definitely not hard to do. If you manage to get them in the area of the screen then they're gonna get zapped... All around I was nicely surprised with how well these work.

reviewTitle: I STILL USE MY OLD FASHIONED FLY SWATTER

Nor does it catch any flies when I wave it slowly, like one of the reviewers claims. If a fly finds it's way to the screen, it will be stunned, possibly electrocuted. But I still get lots of flies inside my house, even though I leave two of these plugged in their stands (NOT chargers, though they might look that way in photos) in the kitchen and living room. Why didn't I return them? I had used them a few times and I can't be sure other brands will be different.

reviewTitle: Super Ninja Fruit Fly Traps: bait useless for my fruit flies while Terro brand bait has worked well.
reviewDescription: I tried 3 different brands of liquid fruit fly attractant: in this home for 25 yrs but had never had a fruit fly problem in my life, they must have come in w/fruit which we don't buy a lot of: found out one female lays 500 eggs...This product, Super Ninja Fruit fly traps- I put several of these pre-filled bottles out for days but SUPER NINJA FRUIT FLY TRAPS DID NOT CATCH A SINGLE FRUIT FLY, compared to Terro brand used in the red plastic tomatoes, which worked well. I even tried the Super Ninja bait in the red Terro plastic tomatoe containers as some ads claimed the color + shape of the vessel played a role in attracting bugs but still no luck. The excellent Terro brand even worked well in a clear glass baby food jar. Terro works well, I see several of the fruit flies in the liquid. However, the tomatoe shaped plastic holders can tip easy, its a sticky, stinky liquid for which the company in South America would not tell me if it has a percent of poison in it- many of their products do, + they only give the number of poison control, + insist that the formula is 'proprietary information'. But after trying 3 brands, will stick w/Terro even though it does not sell a large container of liquid (I do not need more plastic tomatoe holders plus glass baby food jars worked just as well) Have put the Terro liquid bait-filled plastic tomatoes in clear plastic cups (clear so insects could see the red fruit shape), as the tomatoes have many preferations, which is good, but they are light-weight, tip + spill easily. However, the flat rectangular shape of the bottles of the Super Ninja Fruit fly Traps, though the liquid they came with is useless, the bottles w/their flat walls can be attached to any flat surface or wall w/a sticky strip; That had been my intent when I ordered them so I did not have to worry about containers spilling or space to put them down in or my cat getting to one... As the Ninja contents have proved useless, I will empty + rinse them out so I can fill them w/Terro attractant. will report if the insects will use these glass containers. While I had planned to take advantage of the flat walls of the Ninja glass containers + a photo in the Ad may have suggested they could be stuck to a wall, I was surprised that they came w/a pre-attached silicone sticky strip pre-attached for that purpose (one should use a spatula to remove the sticky strip and bottle intact from the wall (they are silicone w/adhesive + residue can be easily removed). There was one other brand of 25 vials which also did not catch a single fruit fly- even when used in Terro's tomatoe shape, which I also do not recommend, 'Qualirey 20 Pack Fruit Fly Trap Refill Liquid'... Do not recommend the Super Ninja Fly Traps because the bait does not work, at least for my fruit flies in Connecticut; but I still need to substitute Terro liquid bait in their their rectangular glass containers a+ see if the insects will then use them before I formally recommend the containers themselves- they have a yellow funnel on top. However, $39.99 plus tax is a high price to pay for the 12 glass containers which I will need to empty out to use with Terro brand liquid- if the bugs will use that container w/Terro bait.

reviewTitle: Not very effective for fruit flies
reviewDescription: I recently went out of town for a week and left the wife on her own. We already had a couple fruit flies when I left (maybe 3-5 that I noticed), but this isn't unusual since we always buy fruit as it comes into season and this brings fruit fly eggs into the house. Before I left I just reminded her that the trap needed to be setup and she said it wouldn't be a problem. By trap, I mean the apple shaped trap you can get at just about any store where you put a little vinegar and dish soap in and it just "makes the problem go away." I have used those traps for years and they work great, I just put one on the table near the fruit bowl and one near the kitchen sink. Well, I get home and it seems the wife stopped doing dishes and taking out the trash for a week and and fruit flies had gone ham in the sink. Absolute infestation. Swarms plume from the sink when you approach it. The little apples couldn't keep up. So I figured I would try something new. The new UV trap has been setup for 4 days/3 nights. The trap is plugged in right next to the kitchen sink and all the lights are off at night so this trap has had the best chance of succeeding. It has caught, a disappointing number of flies. Maybe 15-20 in that number of days when the number in the house is multiple hundred. I actually just before writing this unplugged it and waved it around over the sink and caught 4 time more in 30 seconds waving this thing around than I have in 4 days. This is probably fine as a very minor preventative, but it costs way more than the little apples, is far more noticeable and not any more effective. I do not recommend.

reviewTitle: Doesn't work or is too slow.
reviewDescription: Look, the idea of trying to get rid of those annoying fruit flies makes my skin crawl and my blood boil at the same time. I hate them. Most insect such as those I despise. But nothing I hate more than fruit flies. This product took AGES to catch even a couple of them, let alone the thousands that are made per day sometimes. If any of you reading this have had them, you know what I mean. They are obnoxious. Here is the thing: the quicker you kill them off, the better. This will not do it, not even close. Left them on for a month and when I went to just toss them in the garbage, I could literally count the fruit flies and other things that somehow found there way behind this to get stuck. Here is how you get rid of fruit flies MUCH QUICKER and for WAY LESS MONEY: Apple cider vinegar + ramekin or other small/medium container + clear plastic wrap + toothpick. Fill half of the ramekin with the vinegar, stretch film over the top. Poke little holes in the saran wrap. Place near infected area. After a day or two, you will be unable to count how many of those little ******* are dead in there. Rinse and repeat when it starts to get too full. I have probably killed millions with this little death pit. These are a gimmick, stay away. Especially for fruit flies. Not sure how well they work for mosquitos.

reviewTitle: Apple cider vinegar works better than this!
reviewDescription: This doesn’t even deserve 1 star. It’s officially been 24hrs since it was delivered and plugged in. It has been in my kitchen, directly next to the fruit that sits on the counter. I currently have an INSANE amount of fruit flies due to “someone” leaving an opened applesauce in the pantry…. I did a test. I put a small medicine cap filled with apple cider vinegar and Dawn dish soap in a separate portion of my house, where I have seen the least activity of fruit flies. The kitchen area is over run with fruit flies. The photos speak for themselves. For fun, I even took a piece of cardboard and did a layer of Elmer school glue right after I plugged in the blue light. Again, the photos speak for themselves. You see how many fruit flies were trapped by the Elmers glue makeshift trap, in the short time it took to dry, compared to this $20 piece of junk. I truly don’t understand how this has so many great reviews. Photo 1 is the small cap filled with ACV and Dawn (the cloudiness are drowned fruit flies). I recently purchased the ZEVO Fly Trap after reading numerous glowing reviews that touted its effectiveness in catching flies. With summer in full swing and the usual influx of pesky insects, I was eager to try out this product. Unfortunately, my experience has been far from the exceptional results I had hoped for. Performance: Over the course of a week, I placed the ZEVO Fly Trap in a strategic location where I typically see a lot of fly activity. To my disappointment, it only caught one fly during that entire time. I had expected a much higher catch rate based on the reviews I had read, which made me question the reliability of those testimonials. Expectations vs. Reality: Given the rave reviews, I anticipated a significant reduction in the fly population in my home. Instead, I found myself swatting flies as usual, with the trap seemingly doing little to help. It’s puzzling how others have had such positive experiences while mine was lackluster at best. Conclusion: While the ZEVO Fly Trap may work for some, my experience has been disappointing. I had high hopes based on the exceptional reviews, but the results did not match the hype. If you’re considering this product, I recommend tempering your expectations and perhaps exploring other options if you’re dealing with a significant fly problem. For me, it’s back to the drawing board in the search for an effective fly control solution.

reviewTitle: In FIVE days it only caught THREE FLIES - Sending it back
reviewDescription: I am underwhelmed. In FIVE days it only caught THREE flies. The old fashioned ugly-ass fly strips that are coated with the sticky attractant are just a few dollars for a pack of ten that will last several months and they work better than this useless contraption. Very disappointed. All those 5 stars Amazon reviews must be AI generated? Right now I am sitting on my couch in my living room and there are 8 flies buzzing around me driving me CRAZY. Flies are strongly attracted to carbon dioxide that humans breathe out. They will land on us and eat the dead skin cells and sebum on our skin. No matter how much you swat them away they keep coming back. This gadget looked impressive - the blue light is supposed to attract the fly into the sticky trap BUT THAT DOES NOT WORK except in a very minimal ineffectual manner. Seriously! For what this costs to only catch THREE flies in FIVE days is unacceptable. I did better putting out a dish of vinegar with sugar as an attractant but put a tbsp of Dawn dish soap in the bowl - that attraxts the fly and they drown. I'm sending it back for a full refund. I also bought a box of refills and that goes back to Amazon. BADLY DONE! 1 star is too high a rating

reviewTitle: These are worthless. Do not buy them.
reviewDescription: DO NOT waste your money on this product. I have had two of these since September. The picture is after 6 months. There is nothing but cat hair. I always have an issue when I bring home fruit. I usually wash it but that still does not guarantee no gnats. The best way to get rid of gnats is this. I get a quart bottle of cider vinegar. I put 2tbs of sugar, and 5 drops of dish detergent in the bottle. The detergent changes the viscosity of the mix so once they get in they can't get out. Put a piece of saran wrap on the top and hold it in place with a rubber band. Poke 3 or 4 holes in the top and leave it next to the source or close to the area that is infested with gnats. I thought these would be great as my wife hates the vinegar smell. But they do nothing. It's a great night light, but I have fairy lights for night lights. Don't waste your time or money on this product if you are trying to kill gnats. I don't know how this product works with mosquitoes, as I have no mosquito problem in the house.

reviewTitle: Does not attract or trap fruit flies--dish soap or plug-in light works much better
reviewDescription: DOES NOT WORK. I have a gnat infestation in my indoor office plants. Because they are in my office, where I spend 8 hours per day, I needed them gone, stat. I tried 3 different methods of gnat elimination: (1) Terro apple-shaped fly traps; (2) Shallow dishes of liquid dish soap; (3) Zevo plug-in flying insect trap. After 3 days, here are the results: (1) Terro apple-shaped fly traps: Easy to set up and fill with their "special" gnat-attracting formula which I am sure is just apple cider vinegar. The apple containers are small and cute. But, they don't work. I CAUGHT 1 (ONE) GNAT using 4 different traps placed on top of soil in my plant containers. (2) Shallow dishes of dawn dish soap: Easy to set up and fill. However, the liquid soap starts to dry out and harden after several days. They are a bit of a pain to wash out and refill, icks me out, plus I think it's a waste of dish soap. I CAUGHT 50 GNATS using this method--I used 4 different shallow containers placed on top of soil in my plant containers. (3) Zevo plug-in flying insect trap. This plug-in fly trap was super easy to set up. It's basically a blue light that attracts the insects and super sticky paper (comes with replacement inserts) to trap them. I CAUGHT 40 GNATS using this method. This trap is the furthest from my plants--it's about 6 feet away. If I move it closer, it will probably work even better. This is my favorite scent-free, mess-free method that actually works. If I didn't use the other 2 methods, I would have about 100 more gnats still flying around my office space, ick. Save your money and don't buy.

reviewTitle: Doesn't work for "fungus flies" - tiny black fliers...

Would not recommend for this type of invasion. Next step will be throwing away all the house plants and starting over.

In the past, there are multiple fruit flies floating in the apple within the first 24 hours and, at minimum, multiple fruit flies congregating on the surface of the "apple." Or did they change the formula this year? Maybe I received a bad batch? Who knows. Until next summer when the fruit flies come back.

reviewTitle: GREAT AT GNATS - NOT SO MUCH WITH FLIES
reviewDescription: Okay. Last I reviewed these or similar as I have used both Zevo lamps and stickers and off-brand lamps and stickers. I’ve used off-brand stickers in Zevo and I’ve used Zevo stickers in off-brand lamps. Results as follows….. No matter what you use, brand name or non-brand name, or in any combination, this product is 4 stars for fruit flies / gnats and about 2.75 - 3.0 stars for any other flying insects. It attracts and catches all - mosquitoes, flies, gnats / fruit flies, and other unknown flying insects however, gnats are its specialty. Clearly, my personal experience is stated here. I have a 2 story with about 1,000sf on the ground floor. With 2 units in the kitchen, 2 around my plants, and another in the bedroom I have a flying bug free environment. I use and highly recommend any. Although I have noticed side-by-side the Zevo lamps are a bit brighter but I can tell no difference in the amount of insects trapped with off-brand. Also, as stated (at least by Zevo) they work best when entire rooms can be darkened for the lamp to have the most effect. reviewTitle: Attracts some but not all species. Liquid bait traps are better.
reviewDescription: About a month ago I suddenly and inexplicably noticed dozens of tiny little bugs flying around in my kitchen. Absolutely disgusting. Neurotic cleaning ensued, and, eventually, the flies dissipated, but not as quickly as I would have liked. I decided to purchase some fly traps to put my thumb on the scale. These light traps appeared to be working at first. They were definitely catching some flies, but the total number of flies was not visibly decreasing. Then I noticed that the flies stuck on the trap looked different than the ones still flying around. The light was definitely attracting one species of fly, but another species seemed completely unaffected. They were a lighter color, whereas the flies in the trap were darker. After a few days, the darker flies were gone, and the lighter flies appeared to have expanded their population to take advantage of their absence. So anyways, I suppose whether these traps work for you will depend on what species of fly you’re dealing with. For me, these traps were ineffective. I then purchased some of those liquid bait traps that look like tiny little apples, and they worked incredibly well. The liquid comes in a tiny little bottle and cannot be purchased separately, which is asinine, but apple cider vinegar with a little dish soap appears to work just as well as the bait that comes with the trap. I tried using apple cider vinegar with dish soap in a DIY trap before purchasing some traps, but this was not effective. I think it was too difficult for the flies to get to the bait. The premade traps with DIY bait appears to be the best of both worlds.

However, after several days with the waste fruit on the kitchen countertop - less than two feet away - there are a dozen or two fruit flies stuck in the glue, but more than that are still just flying around. Results over a longer time remain to be seen. The fruit scraps are in a "Green Waste" bucket. When it is dark and all other lights are off, I aggressively tap the sides of the container several times to give the fruit flies another excellent opportunity to get attracted to the blue light and get stuck.

reviewDescription: The set I received wasn't functioning properly. The cartridge cover, when clicked fully in place, is supposed to create the blue light when plugged in. Mine just wasn't connecting properly -- but rather than try returning it (because I needed immediate fruit fly help) I used packing tape to create the needed tension to lock the cover in place. And it worked...at least for a few days until the tape popped off. I've since retaped, and it continues to work. And when checked, the adhesive had collected many of the little bugs...so I'm struggling through with it because it's getting the job done. At some point, I'll spring for a new mainframe.
After about a week of installing the Zevo, I started to see its worth. I was expecting better results but at some point, the gnats find their way in. I’ll buy again when needed.

reviewTitle: Not so great for Phorid Flies. Otherwise, probably good.
reviewDescription: Well, it did get some very tiny gnats I was not aware were in the house, and a house fly. But I've been dealing with what I believe are Phorid Flies. And, 2-3 weeks of use and not a one. They are literally flying 15-20 inches from this and ignore it. I'm truly sure this is good for some things. Has to be, cool light and all that should attract bugs. Sad as I was told this was the key weapon for these particular pests, but Umm... Nope. For the record, I've turned off every light in the house but this unit, I've also tried subdued lighting in the house as it seems these things settle in and sleep if it's dark. I put a piece of white paper behind it to really reflect the light better. No joy. Seems whatever I have, are really only motivated by food smells. Not lights, not wine or vinegar, I truly cannot find what (organic matter" they are reproducing in. Which obviously is the base issue. I'm going mad with these things. Pheromones or the 65 dollar floor drain flush are next to try I guess. Never seen these things in my life haha... I move from the inner city to the suburbs, and here we are. lol

reviewTitle: Why Produce More Plastic than Necessary
reviewDescription: This product works. But when you buy a refill, you toss the plastic shield and the thin layer of bug killer attached to it, thereby requiring yet another plastic shield to push into the device after sticking the bug killer to it. I just cannot see myself using this much plastic. I imagine resin could be used to make the shield and a thin layer of resin to which the sticky killer could be attached. To require the customer buy refills that are in a box with only 2 refills per box is asking a bit much. If one were able to buy 10 thin layers of resin with just a thin layer of resin one could then pull off and attach to the original it would be so much more sustainable. In the commercial, the person chucks the whole thing in the trash…just such waste. More thinking needs to be done on the materials used.

reviewTitle: Best for larger bugs, not fruit flies
reviewDescription: We bought this because it had so many amazing reviews, specifically for fruit flys. We went on vacation and sadly forgot to take out the recycling, when we got home our kitchen was filled with fruit flies. We used one of those electric swatters and got SO MANY, but there were still creeps lingering, so I bought this product, for two rooms (1000 sq ft apartment), and we have caught exactly one fly, but an actual, large one. So we still run around chasing fruit flies with our racket. So maybe it's great if you have the big guys, I don't recommend for the little ones.

I’ve seen ads for this product for a while so it immediately came to mind as a solution when we started having a fruit fly problem. I like the concept of this product but felt it wasn’t as effective as it should have been. I placed it where I had seen most of the fruit flies hanging out and it did catch a few, but I was a little disappointed that it wasn’t making much of a dent in the population. Something else I should have considered before purchasing was how brightly the blue light shines from this device. We avoid blue light around bedtime and dim the lights in our house in the evenings as we get ready to sleep and so this didn’t fit with my personal sleep hygiene preferences - but that’s honestly my fault for not thinking through how this product works before purchasing it. After giving Zevo a chance for a few days I decided to try the old apple cider vinegar in a jar with tiny holes poked in the lid method. The apple cider jar was the clear winner and captured at least 3-4 times more in a single night than the Zivo had in days. The homemade method was much cheaper too. If I could go back and do it all over again I would just do the jar trick and save money by not purchasing this product.
Not sure they are doing any good. We continue to see the same little gnats.

I'm not impressed. I live in the county, have a house dog, and flies. The minute I open my door, a couple of flies come in. So, I bought two of the large-size Zevo's. Installed them in my kitchen, as that seems to be where the flies go. I have captured 3 flies in these Zevos in the last 2 weeks. I have fly swattered many more than that. I don't want to chase flies around with a fly swatter. That's why I bought these Zevos. I feel that I wasted my money. My neighbor has good luck with her Zevos catching gnats. I don't have gnats. The Zevo has a good rating. So it's catching something. But I don't think it's flies.

reviewTitle: Didn’t Attract or Catch Many Gnats
reviewDescription: I was hopeful about the Zevo Flying Insect Traps, but unfortunately, they didn’t work as well as I expected. I followed all the setup instructions and placed them where gnats are usually a problem, but they barely caught any. The light is supposed to attract flying insects, but it didn’t seem to draw in the gnats in my home. After several days, there were only a few bugs stuck to the cartridge. For the price, I was disappointed with the results. On the plus side, it’s easy to set up and looks clean and safe around kids and pets—but effectiveness was lacking for gnats or fruit flies in my case. I might try a different brand or stick with natural traps next time.

reviewDescription: The description states 2 Plug-in Bases + 2 Refill Cartridges. You expect that with the 2 Plug-In Bases you would get the Cartridges and 2 refills. You only get the bases no cartridges in the bases. The description is very tricky to read as your expecting the Plug-In bases to have everything to just plug the unit in and it will contain the fly trap. It does not come ready to use, you have to insert the "Refill Cartridge", so you only get 2 Plug-ins and no inserts, you have to use your refills, only then is it ready to use, the description is deceptive. In my opinion its shorting you 2 cartridges. In order to resolve this "Description Issue", Amazon or the Seller should change the description to "Zevo Flying Insect Trap, Fly Trap, Fruit Fly Trap (2 Plug-in Bases + 2 (Refill should be removed), Cartridges). This way the consumer will get a complete product and without "Refills".

reviewTitle: It's the Amperage not the Voltage
reviewDescription: This product does work, but remember, it's the amount of amperage that kills, not the voltage. IME, this 4,000 voltage fly swatter doesn't have enough amperage to instantly kill most house flys unless they get entangled in wiring. The flies need to be zapped up to three times to kill them. But if you hate flies in the house, the zapping noise is satisfying perversely - you still need to hunt them down. UPDATE: After using it more, I am revising my rating to 4 stars. As a stand-alone unit for house flies, it is useless - not bright enough for flies and only seems to stun them. Swinging widely at flying flies doesn't work well because if you by chance hit them and they are not stuck to the racket, they are merely stunned. If you have flies on the windows - it does work better than a fly-swatter as you don't have a mess on the window.

Unless you actually get them stuck on the swatter and they fry up, it doesn’t really kill them on the first hit which is annoying bc though sometimes it slows them down you still have to hit them an extra 2-3 times before they are actually down.

The "ZAP" noise will make almost anyone jump. It is unsettling loud for no good reason. I would NOT recommend to use this as a "fly swatter". Nor would i want to leave it sitting anywhere and on as the random loud zap noise will break your focus on what ever else you may be doing each time it goes off. Also, had a small moth fly into it and it started smoking pretty badly.

reviewTitle: Great until it started fly bbq
reviewDescription: It worked great and I especially liked the stanby option... until fly guts got trapped inside the inner electrical wires, now every time I turn it on it creates a putrid smell of bbq fly. There is no way to clean it since you can't take off the screen. Now I don't use it cause it makes a nasty smell.

Duel purpose swatter, in a stand with blue lite on it attracks flys which seems to work well, problem is it isent strong enough to kill them, just knocks Them out temporarily. I have watched then get up and fly away. The charger should be in the stand rather then plugging in the side of the handle. I would not purchase this again. I don't want to knock them out I want to kill them

I was going to return it. It was not consistent. One would work while the other would not. Then it would keep sparking and make so much noise that it warns the bugs to get out of the way. But since I have used it to kill some bugs, I decided to just keep it. I still should have returned it.

The product is actually pretty cool. We are only giving it three stars now because of the product itself, but to bring attention to any dog owners out there that when this zapper zaps a bug, it scares the heck out of our dog. Literally terrifies him. He starts trembling and won’t stop for several minutes. So, we gave it away to a neighbor. That said, I wish the dog didn’t care because it’s quite satisfying killing bugs with this thing. If you’re not concerned about a pet freaking out at the sound of it, definitely give it a try

I really liked it for the first few weeks and then all of a sudden it started sparking/shocking when nothing was even flying into the swatter. Definitely didn’t seem safe to use anymore. Little disappointed. Only had the product about a month

It attracted and killed a few flies. If you have a real fruit fly problem, the Terro Fruit Fly Trip along with the DEVOGUE Electric Fly Swatter Bug Zapper are your best bets. They work well together.

Didn't work as well as I thought it would, I even tried moving it around. I know I have fruit flies, gnats and mosquitoes flying around. The trap has only a few bugs on it. Maybe it's too old. When I received the package it looked like it had been opened and resealed, but it wasn't a good job if resealing. The box was also bent. My order was late, so I had 2 name stickers on mine, one on top of the other one. I saw on the news when a Amazon truck was involved in a highway pile up accident. I wondered if that was the reason my order looked like someone had it for a long time and decided that they didn't want it anymore and returned it.
For fruit flies, it’s just ok. It was easily outperformed by a DIY set up of rotting fruit with a Saran Wrap cover and small poke holes. It caught some, just not enough to justify the money.

Save your money! We had a minor infestation of fruit flies in the kitchen, so we bought this on the recommendation of a friend. My wife got a cheaper solution off the internet to use a bowl half filled with apple cider vinegar covered with plastic wrap with some holes poked it in for the bugs to fly into & not get out. We captured way more bugs with that then the expensive plug-in.

I have a few extremely small flying bugs in my home. I don't know where they came from, and I can't seem to get rid of them. I bought this product thinking the blue light attracted them, and the product somehow killed them. I have seen no evidence at all that the blue light attracts the bugs. If a bug gets caught in the device, it's totally random as far as I can tell. Also, behind the nice looking cover, it's just a sheet of sticky paper. The bug lands on the sticky paper an can't fly off, slowly starving to death.

reviewTitle: You would be better off catching them by hand.
reviewDescription: Im giving this stuff 1 star cuz I cant give it minus 1…Ive had a fairly large Fruit Fly problem….Using the Terro Apples ,I get 30-50 per day,,at present the best,with the lighted,sticky traps,,,This entire pack lured Zero Flies…After chasing my tail for a month ,trying to find where they were breeding,,not my sinks ,toilets or trash can..I plugged or covered all of them…I finally hit on where,,,my Cat Litter..not the Clay stuff I hate ,but my fav of all..Worlds Best ,which is made from organic material..I tried cleaning them twice a day ..no luck..I had to put them outside for the duration….They can go from egg to breeding in as little as 7 days and live for 30…Gotta remove the food source and wait them out,,The cats will just need to adapt…spoiled little snots,,

reviewDescription: This is a terrible product. I bought 4 of these and then my wife bought another 4 unbeknownst to me. Will these kill fruit flies? Absolutely. So why the 1 star review? They'll make more fruit flies than they'll kill. They don't kill off all of the fruit flies. The ones that survive reproduce and make even more fruit flies. We went from having a dozen to dozens of dozens. Our kitchen is crawling with them. The traps allow the fruit flies to move in and out freely so the flies that don't die just reproduce more resistant flies. How did I end up fixing this Terro created problem? By making my own fruit fly traps with jars filled with red wine, plastic wrap over the top with a rubber band to hold it in place, and then poking holes through them. Cost me nothing since I had these objects already on hand. I will never buy this product again.

These traps are so tiny that you cannot grasp them to get the top off. The top is a pop off top but you can’t get it off. Have husband find a thin metal tool and perhaps he can pry it off. The attractant smells like vinegar and you can squirt it thru the top hole; however not sure how you can pry the top off to clean it. Previously I have used the FlyFix Fruit fly trap. It is bigger and the top screws off for cleaning and filling. I have always used apple cider vinegar with excellent results. They are a little more expensive but worth it if you have fruit fly problems. No need to keep buying attractant as apple cider vinegar works well; some people put slices of banana in the trap and claim it works well. I’m going to try it! I just ordered some more FlyFix fruit fly traps.

reviewTitle: Fruit Fly Killer vs. Home Remedy (FTW)
reviewDescription: I bought the fruit fly traps and set them out... nothing. Nor do I see the fruit flies interested in the little traps after several days. They're circling my hanging pans more than the little trap. Maybe the solution was expired? I got online and found some homemade remedies. I put out several plates with apple cider vinegar mixed with sugar and dish soap. Worked like a charm. Two days later, lots of fruit flies in that mixture and problem is down 80%. For pennies on the dollar, a much better solution. I'm going to try the homemade solution inside their cute little traps to see if it works. After 2 weeks: I kept both the traps out and the homemade remedy. Traps caught about 20% of the flies; homemade remedy on plates about 80%. Also, the liquid in the traps dried out quick and were a pain to refill, etc.
Don’t waste your money. This didn’t work at all!

This product is not good. It created a medium for the fruit flies to mass reproduce. Now I have a small infestation. Very upset about this product

reviewTitle: Make Sure You Have Fruit Flies Not Gnats
reviewDescription: I thought I had fruit flies but I think I had gnats. These did not trap anything and the liquid provided just evaporates. Amazon would not take our return as you have to open the box to see the product. We then ordered the product that you plug into the wall that shines a blue light and captures gnats on a sticky surface. This second product is working beautifully. We have caught over 100 gnats in a few days. So, make sure you have fruit flies not gnats. Fruit flies and gnats look very similar, so don't make the same mistake we did, or you will be wasting your money like we did.

reviewTitle: NEVER EVER BUY THIS! WASTE OF MONEY!
reviewDescription: DO NOT BUY LIKE SERIOUSLY ITS SO BAD! I had a fly infestation and the liquid did nothing but evaporate (it was indoor too). reviewTitle: Used for years, but something changed!
reviewDescription: I’ve been brand-loyal to Terro for years. When I opened the box, I noticed new packaging for the liquid- and now I notice that it no longer traps flies like it used to. With the old bait (clear cylinder, blue screw lid), I would catch ALL of them. This packaging (clear squeeze tube with lid that needed to be cut off) must contain a different formulation because it doesn’t work at all. It also evaporates more quickly.
This did not work well. I had better results with vinegar and dish soap. The apples are cute, but that's about it.

These are crap. They definitely attract flies but there’s so little liquid that if the flies don’t go all the way in the trap and down the the very bottom, nothing’s going to happen. Them advertising the flies have no way back out is literally just false. The holes they go in through are so large they can just as easily get back out. Going into the little liquid given is the only thing that kills them.

This was a super easy to put together and aesthetically pleasing fly catcher, the only problem is that it doesn’t catch any flies. There’s not a single fruit fly in the container and although they seem to be hovering around the area—I’ve kept this catcher out for about half of the week and absolutely nothing. Save yourself some dollars and make a catcher yourself with sugar and apple cider vinegar.

Edit: Knocking off two more stars because the liquid evaporated in 2 days. 180 days? Nope. Noticed improvement within 12 hours, but only after placing two traps, one did not seem to be enough. I will also add that the solution seems to be your standard apple cider vinegar. If the apple trap is what you want specifically over a jar and saran wrap it's definitely worth it but otherwise there's nothing special about the product. Be sure to fill the apples away from any spot you want to save, I filled them at my work desk before moving them where they were needed and that was my mistake. The lingering smell of vinegar attracted them to that spot for hours and it was quite annoying.

These are simply tiny plastic containers filled with apple vinegar. Absolutely zero accountability or pride in their product. Buying these is identical to buying snake oil. Just but apple cider vinegar and sage your money. -CSL

reviewTitle: Not the Terro Quality I Expected
reviewDescription: Terro Ant baits are my go-to for dealing with ant problems, so I assumed this product would work just as effectively for dealing with fruit flies. WRONG. The flies go in the plastic apple, but are able to fly right out. This product caught zero fruit flies. In contrast, our homemade fruitfly trap that we keep at the other end of our home behind a closed door works like a charm. We took an empty salsa glass jar (the kind that is short with a wider opening and is generally found next to the potato chips at the grocery store), and poked several small holes in the metal lid (I forgot whether we used a nail, or a drill), and filled it halfway with apple cider vinegar. Works like a charm and cost almost nothing to make. I bought the Terro fruitfly traps for the kitchen because I'm too lazy to make another homemade trap, and I thought the plastic apples would look nicer. But it ended up being a waste of money.
Doesn't seem to attract our flies as expected. After a week only 1 fly trapped. Multiple flies are present but they don't appear interested in this trap

reviewTitle: If you have houseflies this will make it much worse
reviewDescription: TLDR - if you have homeflies in your area, don't buy this. This was the biggest mistake I've made in years buying this product. If you have a fruit fly problem, buy a plastic cup and fill it with apple cider vinegar; it's the same thing. I live in an area with houseflies, this product made it so much worse. The smell of the apple cider vinegar attracted hundreds of houseflies. They actually started hanging around my door and finding the tiniest cracks to funnel through. They crawled through the screen door via the corners, through cracks in window AC units, etc. I spent hours catching them and had to buy a electric fly swatter. Its one thing to buy a product that doesn't work, its something completely different to create more problems and worsen it. Plus its already a way overpriced for a container and apple cider vinegar.
Not worth it. Ive left two traps for over 2 days and they’ve catched maybe 4 fruit flies all together. The flies literally stay on top of the plastic and dont fall inside. Not very effective
Useless product, in over 3 days of use, it only trapped a single gnat, while a swarm of it is flying around it

These do not work. Today I went back to my old method of apple cider vinegar and dish soap. I had more fruit flies in two hours than in 5 days with this product. Yes, they look cute, but a waste of money. I think it's because the surface area is too small for the flies to get attracted. The jar I'm using is completely open and works great.

THIS DOES NOT WORK! The one thing it’s supposed to do…..AND the bugs are ACTUALLY MOCKING ME. I don’t know who came up with this idea, or what research went in that says this is effective…. The catch ability is NON-EXISTENT!! As seen in the picture, they don’t die or get caught.

There are too many holes, and they are much too large. The fruit flies easily go in and out of the trap. The liquid evaporates too fast to last 30 days. The liquid was gone in just 2 weeks and only caught about 4 bugs in that amount of time. I then used my grandmother's version of a fruit fly trap using a small relish jar with 2 tiny holes drilled in the lid. The solution is apple cider vinegar. In just the 2weeks the Terror trap was in place, the homemade trap caught 2 dozen fruit flies & the liquid hasn't evaporated. In 30 days, the whole surface of the liquid in the glass jar was covered in fruit flies.

Frankly unimpressed. Before getting these, I used a bowl with a TBS of sugar, half a cup of warm water and a few tablespoons of apple cider vinegar with a few drops of Dawn. I also have three Zevo insect traps (basically very sticky plastic inside a plug in that has a warm blue light). The bowl traps were far more effective at getting a dozen of the buggers trapped (fruit fly invasion, and I do mean invasion). The "bait" in these apple traps obviously contains apple cider vinegar. But the flys would crawl around on the outside and only a few would actually end up in the trap. These traps are very small, and supposedly last 45 days which is ridiculous. After only a few days, they stopped working completely. The Zevo traps, on the other hand, had well over a hundred flies trapped. I had to change those traps out twice a week (it's normally like a month or two before they have to be changed, but INVASION of fruit flies). They are much more effective.
Didn't work as I expected it would. went back to a bowl of apple cider vinegar and a few drops of dish soap, and it seems to work a lot better.

The lure liquid seems to be just vinegar, which doesn’t appear to attract the fruit flies as expected. A sweeter scent might have worked better. After giving it a fair try, I cannot recommend this product. It did not deliver the results I needed, and I would suggest looking for a more effective solution for your fruit fly problem.

reviewTitle: Greenway traps are better than Terro
reviewDescription: Greenway traps each caught a dozen fruit flies in a couple of days. The only downside is that they don't catch other species of small bugs. (For those pour bleach down the drain.) My Terro traps caught only a few before the liquid evaporated. The Greenway bottle design keeps them from spilling, and also from evaporating. Terro traps have several big holes so they can spill, and that also allows the liquid to evaporate faster. Terro comes with refills but Greenway doesn't need them. The bugs can also get out of the Terro as easily as getting in, but the Greenway traps hold them until the fumes kill them. They can't climb out with the funnel opening for the same reason it doesn't spill.
Mildly effective, but didn't fully get rid of the fruit flies. Just culled their numbers for a bit until they learned how to walk out.

reviewTitle: Cute, convenient containers. But it's just vinegar and dishwashing detergent that didn't work alone.
reviewDescription: Love the cute, convenient containers. But it's literally just vinegar and dishwashing detergent that didn't work alone, and that's what I had tried on my own without much success before buying 4 of these traps for $14. After an entire day of catching NO fruit flies with these, I looked at some YouTube videos and saw a guy who tried BALSAMIC vinegar instead of white vinegar or apple cider vinegar (which is what I had used.) I added the Balsamic vinegar to the Terro solution and left the house to run some errands. When I returned just an hour later, one of the traps already had 10 flies in it. By last night (24 hours later) there were almost 20 in one of them and 10 in another. I'm hoping that by tonight our bizarre infestation will be almost eradicated. But it's the BALSAMIC VINEGAR that I added that made it effective. I don't regret my purchase because these containers were worth the initial investment. My brother said that these used to have a pheromone in them to attract fruit flies but apparently from the reviews, they changed the formula over the past couple of years, and there was nothing in the ingredient list besides the detergent and vinegar.
Easy to use, but they didn’t do much to reduce the fruit flies in my kitchen. Might work for light issues, but didn’t help my situation much.
My dead Venus flytrap killed more flies than this stupid ass fly trap. You'll have a higher fly kill count by just hunting them yourself with your bare hands.

This honestly does not work don’t waste time or money The light is a blue color and i had it on for 2 days and hasn’t caught one fruit fly I did the old apple cider and soap works way better . Just stick to that
